methods.description: Process for training and testing bees in a controlled foraging arena to study their foraging behavior and preferences.

steps.description: All training and testing took place in a foraging arena (L × W × H, 82 cm × 60 cm × 60 cm) painted gray on floor and sides to provide a neutral background.
steps.searchable_summary: set up foraging arena with neutral background

steps.description: Bees were always individually trained and tested.
steps.searchable_summary: train and test bees individually

steps.description: To identify bees suitable for training and testing, 1-4 flower naive individuals were introduced into the arena simultaneously.
steps.searchable_summary: introduce naive bees to arena

steps.description: When a bee landed on a flower in a training or test array, the others were removed from the arena immediately by catching them with vials and returned to the colony.
steps.searchable_summary: remove non-participating bees from arena

steps.description: Bees involved in training were labeled with unique color combinations of acrylic paint after the first training trial, before being returned to the colony.
steps.searchable_summary: label trained bees with paint

steps.description: During assays, a feeder containing 2 M sucrose solution was placed in the center of the foraging arena because some bees would not complete a trial without drinking.
steps.searchable_summary: place sucrose feeder in arena

steps.description: To determine if sucrose feeding affected learning while foraging for pollen, we fed a set of bees on the same type of sucrose solution before the trial began and removed the arena feeder.
steps.searchable_summary: feed bees sucrose before trial

steps.description: Freshly clipped flowers were horizontally displayed (their natural orientation) on custom-built water tubes to prevent desiccation.
steps.searchable_summary: display fresh flowers on water tubes

steps.description: The water tubes were Velcro mounted on a vertical array facing the flight chamber's entrance.
steps.searchable_summary: mount water tubes with Velcro

steps.description: Flowers were arranged in a Cartesian grid with each water tube spaced 7 cm apart in the horizontal and vertical.
steps.searchable_summary: arrange flowers in Cartesian grid

steps.description: In each experiment, we systematically alternated treatments that used the same species pairs in order to control for effects of day on behavior.
steps.searchable_summary: alternate treatments to control for day effects

methods.description: Statistical procedures used to analyze the collected data on bee preferences and behavior.

steps.description: All data were analyzed using R v.3.2.0.
steps.searchable_summary: analyze data using R

steps.description: We used landings for measures of preference.
steps.searchable_summary: use landings to measure preference

steps.description: We used a binomial test to analyze whether naive bees had a preference for one or the other species with their first landing choice.
steps.searchable_summary: perform binomial test on first landing choice

steps.description: To analyze preference across landings for naive bees that visited unrewarding arrays, we used a paired t-test.
steps.searchable_summary: perform paired t-test on landing preferences

steps.description: To analyze the effect of experience on species preference, we used binomial generalized linear mixed effect models (GLMERs).
steps.searchable_summary: use GLMERs to analyze experience effect

steps.description: For these models we included 'BeeID' as a random factor and visits as repeated measures within BeeID.
steps.searchable_summary: include BeeID as random factor in GLMER

steps.description: In cases of significant effects, we ran Tukey's post hoc test to determine which pairs were significant.
steps.searchable_summary: perform Tukey's post hoc test on significant effects

steps.description: For all GLMERs, maximal models were run first. We performed 2 rounds of backward elimination.
steps.searchable_summary: run maximal GLMER models and perform backward elimination

steps.description: To analyze potential interactions between corolla and anther types, we used mixed multinomial logit models (MMNLMs).
steps.searchable_summary: use MMNLMs to analyze corolla and anther interactions
